food_subgroup: Plant-based meat alternatives; mock meat dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani food product class. No specific class exists for mock mutton or vegetarian biryani variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers biryani as a rice-based spiced dish. No specific code for mock mutton or vegetarian biryani; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mock mutton biryani or vegetarian biryani as a clinical food entity.

main_ingredients: mock mutton (textured soy protein or soy-based meat analogue)
whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)
allergens: gluten (possible in some mock meat formulations)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mock mutton biryani with raita is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with soy-based mock mutton pieces and accompanied by a small bowl of raita (spiced yoghurt with cucumber).

nutrient_source.title: Biryani (vegetable/soy-based) — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (biryani rice component) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (textured soy protein, plain yoghurt) with weighted-average calculation for a composite dish
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for mock mutton biryani with raita exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of: (1) cooked basmati rice (~55% by weight, ICMR-NIN), (2) soy-based mock mutton / textured vegetable protein cooked (~25% by weight, USDA SR Legacy item 16120 — Soy protein concentrate, hydrated), and (3) raita (plain low-fat yoghurt with cucumber, ~20% by weight, USDA SR Legacy item 01116). Spices and oil contribute minor macro adjustments. Values represent a plausible central estimate for a restaurant-style serving.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — basmati rice cooked (GI ~58); composite dish GI estimated by dominant carbohydrate source method
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) Am J Clin Nutr
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mock mutton biryani with raita. GI estimated at 58 using the dominant-carbohydrate-source method: basmati rice (cooked) is the primary glycaemic carbohydrate contributor and has a published GI of approximately 58 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The soy protein and yoghurt components lower the effective GI slightly; however, basmati rice GI of 58 is retained as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (58 × 82 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 47.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1240 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 200–250 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per serving (~47.6)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of basmati rice and the large carbohydrate load (~82 g per 400 g serving) produce a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Soy protein content and low saturated fat relative to conventional mutton biryani
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Replacing mutton with soy-based mock meat substantially reduces saturated fat and cholesterol compared to conventional mutton biryani; the soy protein content (~7 g/100 g) may confer a modest LDL-lowering benefit, making this a more cardiovascular-friendly alternative when prepared with minimal ghee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mock Mutton Biryani with Raita is a vegetarian Indian rice dish served at Jewel of India, an Indian vegetarian restaurant in Singapore. It replicates the flavour and texture of traditional mutton biryani by substituting real mutton with soy-based mock meat (textured vegetable protein or soy protein concentrate), which is marinated in yoghurt and aromatic spices before being layered with long-grain basmati rice and slow-cooked using the dum method — a sealed-pot steaming technique that allows the rice and protein to absorb the spiced cooking juices. The dish is fragrant with whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf) and ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder), and is garnished with fried onions, fresh mint, and coriander. Raita — a cooling condiment of plain yoghurt blended with grated cucumber, cumin, and salt — is served alongside to balance the heat of the biryani. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~148 kcal/100 g), meaningful plant protein (~7.2 g/100 g) from the soy component, and complex carbohydrates from basmati rice. Compared to conventional mutton biryani, it is lower in saturated fat and cholesterol. The sodium content is moderate to high (~310 mg/100 g) due to spice blends and cooking salt. The medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load per full serving make portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and is typically halal-certified in Singapore Indian vegetarian restaurants. It is a popular lunch and dinner option and is culturally significant as a festive and celebratory dish in the South Asian community.

How many calories in mock mutton biryani?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for mock mutton biryani with raita exists in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of approved-source entries for cooked basmati rice, hydrated textured soy protein, and plain low-fat yoghurt. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (basmati rice, GI 58) per University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Food name corrected from 'Mock Mutton Biyani' (input spelling) to 'Mock Mutton Biryani' (canonical English spelling); 'Biyani' is a common romanisation variant retained in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no direct laboratory analysis of this dish was found in any approved source. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN biryani rice component entry and was not reliably available across all component sources. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero based on the plant-based and dairy components used; no hydrogenated fats are present in the standard formulation. 6. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice as the dominant glycaemic carbohydrate source; direct GI measurement of this composite dish has not been published. 7. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard Singapore restaurant plate of biryani with raita; actual restaurant portions may vary. 8. Halal status reflects the vegetarian restaurant context; individual diners should verify with the specific restaurant. 9. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for mock mutton biryani in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
